cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The problem is about a knight starting at a1 on a standard 8x8 chessboard and needing to reach h8 in the minimum number of moves. Hmm. I remember that a knight moves in an L-shape: two squares in one direction and then one square perpendicular. So, from any position, a knight can move to up to eight different squares, depending on its current position. First, I should probably visualize the chessboard. The corners are a1 and h8. The knight starts at the bottom-left corner and needs to get to the top-right corner. Since the knight moves in L-shapes, it can jump over other pieces, so there's no obstruction to consider here. I think the best way to approach this is to model the chessboard as a graph, where each square is a node, and each possible knight move is an edge. Then, the problem reduces to finding the shortest path from a1 to h8. This sounds like a classic Breadth-First Search (BFS) problem because BFS is optimal for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph. Let me recall how BFS works.
